text.title: mobile-ux-patterns Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for mobile-ux-patterns. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Use a bottom tab bar for 3-5 main sections of equal importance where users frequently switch between them. Tab bars MUST show icons and labels (iOS HIG: 49pt height; Material Design: 56dp). Icons MUST be 24-28px with at least 8pt label text. One tab MUST be visually active at all times.
rule.rationale: Bottom tab bars support one-handed use on large phones (thumb reaches 60-70% of screen). Research shows 50% less discovery with hamburger menus versus visible tab bars. Always-visible tabs enable instant access without a menu gesture.

rule.statement: Use a hamburger menu for 6 or more navigation items, secondary navigation, or less-frequently-accessed features. Icons MUST be 24x24dp/pt minimum. Menu MUST slide from left, overlay or push content, and close via X button, back gesture, or click-outside.
rule.rationale: Hamburger menus hide navigation behind a gesture, reducing discoverability but conserving screen space. Reserve for secondary or low-frequency sections.

rule.statement: Use content tabs (horizontal scrolling tabs) for 2-5 categories within a single section. Tabs MUST have 48dp/pt height with full-width touch targets. Active tab MUST show a 2-3dp underline indicator. Tabs MAY scroll horizontally if more than 5 exist.
rule.rationale: Content tabs allow fast switching within one section (e.g., Feed, Messages, Notifications within a messaging app). Smaller than bottom tabs, appropriate for subcategories.

rule.statement: For multi-field forms, use progressive disclosure: show only essential fields on each step. Registration MUST not exceed 5 fields per screen. Steps MUST be visually indicated (e.g., "Step 1 of 3") with a back button available on each step.
rule.rationale: Long forms cause abandonment. Progressive disclosure reduces perceived effort and allows users to exit without losing all input. Nielsen found registration abandonment drops from 75% (15 fields) to 30% (5 fields) with progressive steps.

rule.statement: Input fields MUST use appropriate keyboard types: email input triggers email keyboard, phone input triggers numeric keyboard, text area allows text suggestions. Labels MUST remain visible while the keyboard is open.
rule.rationale: Platform-specific keyboards reduce typing errors and accelerate input. Invisible labels force users to scroll up to see what field they are typing in, causing errors and frustration.

rule.statement: Validation MUST occur on blur (user leaves field), not on keypress. Valid fields MUST show a success indicator (green checkmark). Invalid fields MUST show an error message adjacent to the field with specific guidance (e.g., "Email must include @" not "Invalid input").
rule.rationale: Real-time keypress validation interrupts flow and shows errors before users have finished typing. Blur validation allows natural thinking. Specific error messages reduce correction time.

rule.statement: Apply smart defaults where applicable: country auto-detected from IP, phone number formatted by locale, date field shows a date picker, credit card type auto-detected from card number. Users MUST be able to override any smart default.
rule.rationale: Smart defaults reduce friction without removing user agency. Auto-detection is a courtesy, not a requirement.

rule.statement: Pull-to-refresh MUST be implemented for any scrollable list that can have new content. Pull distance MUST be 100-150px with visual feedback (spinner or progress indicator). Refresh MUST complete within 3 seconds on 3G or show a loading skeleton. Cancel if user scrolls back up before refresh completes.
rule.rationale: Pull-to-refresh is a standard iOS and Android gesture. Users expect it in feeds, inboxes, and notification lists. Minimum pull distance prevents accidental refresh during scrolling.

rule.statement: Swipe actions (swipe-left, swipe-right) MUST reveal actions progressively. Full swipe MAY perform an immediate action; partial swipe MUST show action buttons. Destructive actions (delete) revealed by swipe MUST require confirmation or allow undo. Swipe MUST have a clear visual model: preview at 50%, commit at 100%.
rule.rationale: Swipe actions save screen space while maintaining discoverability if buttons appear at partial swipe. Progressive reveal prevents accidental deletion.

rule.statement: Long-press (2+ second hold) MAY open a context menu or activate multi-select mode. On long-press, provide haptic feedback (haptic engine), visual indication (scale or highlight), and a clear exit method (tap elsewhere, Escape). Never hide primary actions behind long-press.
rule.rationale: Long-press is a secondary gesture; primary actions must be visible or one-tap accessible. Haptic feedback signals that something is happening.

rule.statement: For content lists and cards, use skeleton screens (gray placeholder shapes matching final content layout) instead of spinners. Load critical content first (above-the-fold), then defer below-fold content. Skeleton MUST animate at 1-2 second intervals to indicate progress without motion sickness.
rule.rationale: Skeleton screens reduce perceived load time by 20-30% versus spinners (Nielsen research). Users see layout immediately, feel less uncertain. Spinners remain appropriate only for brief operations under 1 second.

rule.statement: Images MUST load lazily below the fold. Pre-fetch the next logical step (next page, next tab) in the background. For truly empty states (first use, user cleared all items, no search results, error), show a friendly message explaining why, a call-to-action for the next step, and optionally an illustration. Do not blame the user ("You have no items") — instead, guide them ("Add your first item").
rule.rationale: Lazy loading and pre-fetching improve perceived speed. Empty state guidance reduces frustration and provides an action instead of a dead end.

rule.statement: Error messages MUST be placed adjacent to the field or action that failed. Message format: [Icon] "What went wrong" [optional reason] [Action button]. Never show error codes ("Error 404") to users—translate to human language ("Page not found"). Message tone must be friendly, never accusatory ("Email must include @" not "You entered an invalid email").
rule.rationale: Proximity reduces scanning time. Human language and friendly tone reduce frustration.

rule.statement: Network errors MUST offer a [Retry] button. Server errors MUST offer [Retry] and [Contact support]. Temporary errors (502) MAY auto-retry with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s) and show a countdown. Permanent errors MUST show a clear message and an alternative path (back to home, try different input).
rule.rationale: Retry buttons give users agency. Auto-retry with countdown prevents thundering herd; countdown shows progress. Alternative paths prevent dead ends.

rule.statement: Primary buttons and interactive elements MUST be at least 44pt (iOS) or 48dp (Material Design) in both width and height. Spacing between touch targets MUST be at least 8pt minimum (preferably 12pt) to prevent accidental taps of adjacent buttons.
rule.rationale: 44-48pt is the minimum thumb-friendly size; research confirms fewer mis-taps and faster completion. Adjacent spacing prevents fat-finger errors.

rule.statement: Text links within paragraphs should be tappable (wrapped in a padded area) rather than line-height only. Minimum link height MUST be 44pt; if height is constrained, increase horizontal padding around the text to meet 44pt clickable area.
rule.rationale: Paragraph text is often 16-18pt; making it fully 44pt tall breaks layout. Padding achieves the touch target without inflating line-height.

rule.statement: Primary actions and CTAs MUST be in the bottom 40-50% of the screen (easy zone, reachable with thumb). Avoid placing critical actions at the top center or top corners on large phones. Secondary actions and less-frequent buttons MAY use the harder-to-reach top 30%. Use the Steve Hoober thumb zone model as reference: easy zone covers bottom, hard zone covers top.
rule.rationale: One-handed use is the default on mobile. The Hoober research quantifies thumb reach; placing primary actions in the hard zone increases miss rate.

rule.statement: Apps MUST support portrait orientation (primary). Landscape orientation SHOULD be supported for content consumption (video, reading). Content MUST NOT be lost on rotation; state MUST persist (e.g., form input, scroll position). Use adaptive layouts: on portrait, stack vertically; on landscape, use two-column layout if space allows.
rule.rationale: Rotation is frequent user behavior, especially on tablets. Losing content or form input on rotation causes frustration and abandonment. Adaptive layouts maximize screen real estate in landscape without breaking mobile portrait experience.
